title: SEGMENT 13 — Target three, nucleic acids: quinolones, rifamycins, metronidazole

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Target three, nucleic acids: quinolones, rifamycins, metronidazole
Third target category: the machinery for handling DNA and RNA.
Three classes here, three distinct mechanisms.
Start with the fluoroquinolones — ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, the "-floxacin" family.

To understand them you need one piece of biology: when a bacterium copies its DNA, the double helix has to unwind, and that unwinding creates torsional strain ahead of the replication fork — imagine twisting a rubber band tighter and tighter.
Bacteria have enzymes that relieve that strain by cutting the DNA, passing strands through, and resealing it.
The key one is DNA gyrase, also called topoisomerase II, and a related enzyme, topoisomerase IV, that helps separate the daughter chromosomes after replication.
These enzymes are essential, and — critically — they are bacterial enzymes; your cells use different topoisomerases, which gives us our selectivity.
Now here's the mechanism, and it's a nasty one for the bacterium.
You might think the fluoroquinolone simply inhibits the enzyme so it can't cut the DNA.
But it's worse than that.
The fluoroquinolone binds the enzyme while the enzyme has the DNA cut open — it traps the complex at the moment the DNA is broken and stabilizes it there, so the enzyme can never reseal the cut.
So instead of just stopping the enzyme, the drug converts the enzyme into a machine that generates lethal double-stranded breaks in the chromosome and then holds them open.
The bacterial genome gets shredded.
That's why fluoroquinolones are strongly bactericidal, and concentration-dependent killers — push the peak high and you trap more complexes and shred faster.
Broad-spectrum, well absorbed orally, very useful drugs.
Their toxicities are mechanistically interesting and clinically topical.

Fluoroquinolones have a black-box warning for tendon damage and rupture, can affect the central nervous system, can prolong the cardiac QT interval, and — note the developmental warning — they're generally avoided in children and pregnancy because of effects on developing cartilage.
Regulators have increasingly urged that they be reserved for situations where there's no better option, precisely because the toxicity profile is broader than was once appreciated.
So: powerful mechanism, real costs.
Second class: the rifamycins, mainly rifampin — a cornerstone of tuberculosis therapy.
Rifampin inhibits the bacterial enzyme that transcribes DNA into RNA: the DNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

It binds to the beta subunit of that enzyme — encoded by a gene called rpoB — and physically blocks the channel through which the growing RNA strand would emerge, so transcription stalls right at the start.
No RNA, no proteins, dead bacterium.
Bactericidal.
And again, selectivity: the bacterial RNA polymerase is structurally distinct from yours, so the drug ignores your enzyme.
But rifampin teaches a crucial resistance lesson that I want you to really absorb.

Because the drug binds one specific spot on one specific protein, a single point mutation in that rpoB gene can change the shape of the binding site just enough that rifampin no longer sticks — and that single mutation confers high-level resistance in one step.
And because bacterial populations are enormous, a resistant mutant is almost always lurking.
So if you treat an active tuberculosis infection with rifampin alone, you will rapidly select out the resistant mutants and fail.
That's why TB is always treated with multiple drugs at once for months, and why we never, ever use rifampin alone for an active infection.
The combination protects each drug from the bacterium's evolution.

Two quick clinical tags: rifampin is a potent inducer of liver P450 enzymes — the mirror image of the macrolide interaction — so it speeds up the metabolism of many other drugs, and it famously turns body fluids — urine, tears, sweat — a harmless orange-red, which is great for confirming whether your patient is actually taking it.
Third, metronidazole — and this one has the most distinctive mechanism in the whole lecture.
Metronidazole is a prodrug: it arrives inert and has to be chemically activated to work.
The activation requires a strongly reducing, oxygen-free environment — the kind found inside anaerobic bacteria and certain protozoa.
Inside those organisms, the drug gets reduced, generating reactive radical intermediates that attack and fragment the organism's DNA.

The genius is the selectivity: only anaerobes and certain protozoa have the reducing machinery to activate the drug, so it's essentially inert in your well-oxygenated human cells and in aerobic bacteria.
It only "switches on" inside the very organisms you're trying to kill.
So metronidazole is the go-to for anaerobic infections and for protozoal infections like giardia and trichomonas.
Its famous clinical pearl: it produces a severe reaction with alcohol — flushing, nausea, vomiting — so patients must avoid alcohol while taking it.
